text: This is a case presentation from the Cleveland Clinic on a robotic intersphincter resection for low rectal neuroendocrine tumor. Fifty-year-old male with past surgical history of nasal surgery, otherwise healthy, who was recently diagnosed with neuroendocrine tumor of the distal rectum was presented to the clinic for further surgical evaluation. Excisional biopsy at the outside hospital showed low-grade neuroendocrine tumor with positive margins. Pathologic findings revealed that there are infiltrating cords of tumor cells in the background of dense fibrous stroma and diffuse strong cytoplasmic expression for synaptophysin.

Colonoscopy performed at our clinic showed a scar tissue from the previous biopsy site. and biopsy results consistent with granulation tissue. Puraoperative pelvic MRI demonstrated enhancing parietal lymph node.

The patient is positioned in modified lithotomy for robotic intersphincter resection of a low rectal neuroendocrine tumor. After confirming no metastatic disease through diagnostic exploration, additional trocars are placed, and the robot is docked from the left side. The robotic arms are positioned, and the patient is placed in right side down and Trendelenburg position.

The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection, identifying and ligating the inferior mesenteric artery before dividing it. Lateral attachments are then taken down, and the splenic flexure is mobilized. The dissection progresses into the rectum.

The surgeon flips the robotic third arm from the right lower quadrant to the left lower quadrant to perform total mesorectal excision down to the pelvic floor. The pelvic dissection is carried around the puborectalis muscles, confirmed by digital examination. Following the completion of the abdominal dissection, the focus shifts to the pelvic approach, where a circumferential mucosal incision is made at the dentate line after placing anal retraction sutures.
After completing the intersphincteric dissection, the surgeon places a wound protector and pulls the bowel through it. A coloanal anastomosis is then constructed using two ovary sutures.

The postoperative recovery was uneventful and patient was discharged to home on postoperative day six. The final pathology revealed The final pathology demonstrated well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor with one metastatic lymph node.
All margins. In conclusion, this case demonstrates the feasibility of the robotic intersphincter resection in a patient with narrow and difficult pelvis.
